note:
2020年春天，搬到硅谷并在科技公司工作的该系毕业生被邀请与目前的研究生分享他们在这个行业获得的经验。
该部门的领导意识到世界已经改变了。在过去的几十年里，毕业生希望留在学术界。在研究生院期间，你接受了学术生涯的培训：你需要做研究，发表论文，做实验，授课，并申请助学金。
情况发生了变化。许多毕业生转行到工业界，谋求软件工程师、数据科学家和机器学习研究人员的职位。可悲的事实是，我的母校根本没有让学生们为过渡做好准备。
在这篇和后续的博客文章中，我将尝试分享我五年前希望知道的信息。
重要提示：这篇博文的目标读者是正在寻找第一份工作的应届毕业生。对于有行业经验的人来说就没那么有用了。
学术界和工业界是不同的泡沫。思维方式、职业变形、生活必需品、项目如何规划、什么是可以接受的、什么是不可以接受的、政治正确的“正常”水平等等，差距是巨大的。
这两个泡沫真的很好地互补了。我喜欢与那些在攻读博士学位时获得学术上的专业变形，并在行业内获得工业专业变形的人一起工作。
在我上一篇博客文章中，我谈到了学者如何略微提高他们编写的代码的可读性。
今天，我将讨论一些即使对那些还没有编写代码的人来说也可能很有趣的事情。这篇博文将是关于钱的。
注1：正文中将有许多$数字。都是税前的！税金会吃掉你薪酬的很大一部分。有一些方法可以将影响降至最低，但我不会谈论这一点。
注2：帖子中的部分链接为推荐链接。我不相信人们可以从写博客中赚钱，这是证明我的假设错误的方法之一：)。
当我在找我的第一份工作时，我需要回答这个问题：“我为我的下一个角色寻找的报酬是多少？”我不知道该说什么。而且我并不孤单。
我的一位朋友，也毕业于我们系，得到了一份工作，HR(招聘人员)问道：“你想要多少钱？”他拿到了2万5千美元的研究生薪酬。乘以3，再加一点，然后告诉她：“80K”。人力资源部想了一会儿，回答说：“我们出10万美元吧。”我认为他可以卖出13-15万美元，然后从那里开始谈判。但他没有做足功课，他对市场的了解还不到连人力资源部的人都感到惊讶的地步。
当你是美国的研究生时，你的年薪大约是25000美元，这是固定的。
当你在这个行业工作时，你计算薪酬的方式会更复杂。
我相信，在你的行业职业生涯之初，最好是在一家大型科技公司工作。在第一部分，我将谈论上市公司的薪酬，稍后我将转到私营公司，也就是所谓的初创公司。
地理。谷歌说，在欧洲从事同样的工作，即使我们谈论的是同一家公司，你的报酬也比旧金山湾区低得多。
你的水平。你经验越丰富，薪水就越高。在大公司，有一种叫做薪酬等级的结构。你的技能与你的等级相关，每个等级都有一个工资等级。更高的档次-更高的薪酬。
结伴。差距并不巨大，但可能会发生这样的情况，即使在旧金山湾区，Netflix为同样的工作支付的费用也会高于亚马逊。
股票市场。你的薪酬中有很大一部分是股权。你们公司的股票涨了，你的工资就高了。它掉下来了，会让你更不开心。
基本工资：固定金额$。对于大多数人来说，每年的费用在10万美元到20万美元之间。
RSU：你可以在股票市场上出售的股票。你的技能越高，你薪酬中的重要部分就越多。
值得一提的是这个问题：“你的出价是多少？”不同于问题：“你赚多少钱？”人们很容易混淆这一点。
在某种程度上，公司会引入正式的员工技能结构。它被称为等级或等级。你会在所有的大公司中看到这一点。不同的公司对评级系统有不同的表示法。fyi提供了从一种表示法到另一种表示法的良好映射。
最被广泛接受的是谷歌、Facebook、Lyft、优步和其他公司使用的那种。
问：那些把大型科技公司作为第一个行业全职职位的人怎么办？他们会被考虑到什么级别？
A：没有博士学位的应届毕业生可以考虑参加L3课程。拥有博士学位的应届毕业生可考虑参加L4级考试。
问：研究人员的工资真的比工程师高吗？A：不，不是。薪酬等级是完全相同的。
问：如果你拥有博士学位，在顶级会议上发表了100500篇论文，然后转行到这个行业，他们会考虑让我上L5级吗？
A：如果你收到报价，那就是L4级的报价。出版物可能会增加你进入面试渠道的机会，但它们不会影响录用中的$。
你可能会升职。你去上高年级。你的基本工资提高了，你还会得到额外的股票奖励。
你可能会拿到奖金。公司根据员工的表现定期给他们发一些钱。通常一年一次，在冬天。
如果你表现好，你会得到一个股票更新。通常一年一次，在冬天。
一名工程师于2019年7月7日加入A公司。她获得了15万美元的基本工资和40万美元的股票奖励，并有四年的归属时间表。四年的归属时间表意味着员工在第一年后只能获得其中的四分之一。RSU不以$计量。它们是以权益单位来衡量的。2019年7月7日，股票行情为每股60美元，她获得了6666股的股票授予。
她预计下一年的工作收入为25万美元=15万美元+(40万美元/4美元)。
2020年7月7日，如果她以当前股价30美元出售RSU，她将获得：
$15万+($400k/4/$60(去年价格))*$30(今年价格)=$200k。
软件工程师接受了对B公司的报价：基本15万美元，并为40万美元的RSU提供为期四年的资助。
$15万+($400k/4/$230(去年价格)*$1389(今年价格))=$754k。
一年前，事情会变成这样，这很明显吗？不怎么有意思。我们知道未来会发生什么吗？不，我们没有。把握股市时机不是一个好主意。
问：我作为博士应届毕业生加入公司，需要多长时间才能晋升到L5级？
答：这要看情况。你没有资格升职。你可能会升职，也可能不会。如果你在3年内没有从L4晋升到L5，你需要考虑另一个职位或另一个公司。希望你能在那里实现你的潜力。
问：L6的薪酬数字看起来很有吸引力。这需要多长时间才能从L5提升到L6？
答：L5级是终点层。90%的员工从来没有超越过它。我们说最好的情况-再过3年。更现实一点--这永远不会发生。
答：作为第一步，你需要得到报价。面试过程竞争非常激烈，没有人喜欢雇佣应届毕业生。世界上没有人知道如何很好地安排面试过程。实力较弱的候选人会得到录用，实力较强的候选人则会被拒绝。大规模招聘优秀人才是一个非常复杂的问题。很难估计在团队与他交谈的几个小时内，候选人的工作效率会有多高。应聘者的实力和他通过面试的能力并不是完全一致的。对于应届毕业生来说，这比对大四学生来说更具随机性。
实习有助于缓解学术界人士的这一问题。在实习期间，应聘者致力于解决与公司面临的问题相近的问题，并与全职员工合作。作为实习的结果，此人可能会得到一份全职工作。大型科技公司提供的应届毕业生绝大多数都是通过实习获得的。
如果你离毕业还有一年多的时间，你需要专注于获得实习机会。它会对你的技能和你的事业有所帮助。作为额外的奖励，它还会在经济上帮助你。在研究生院，你的月薪在2000美元左右，而作为实习生，这个数字将在9000美元/月左右。
问：工作机会中的$$最重要的是什么，是我伟大的出版物，还是我的实习机会。A：实习。
问：如果我能得到一份工作的话。它看起来怎么样？答：只需检查L4的level.fyi，并查找范围的较低部分。
我不是专家。每次我接受录用时，我都觉得招聘人员在操纵我，让我拿到比我能拿到的更低的东西。
我推荐给每个人的一本关于谈判的书，即使你远离求职过程：一切都是可以谈判的：如何每次都能获得最好的交易。
最重要的是获得许多不同公司的报价。这将使你的地位更稳固，并帮助公司了解你的市场价值，这是由外部来源衡量的。
不要先说数字。公司应该这么做。谈判是建立在信息不对称的基础上的。除非你想要，否则你没有义务分享任何东西。
你现在赚多少钱？这在加州是违法的，但招聘人员仍在这么做。
这是我最喜欢的一句话：“能让你接受报价的最低金额是多少？”
所有这些规则都有例外。当人们联系我，建议面试他们的职位时，我会清楚地说明我对薪酬的期望。他们消失了，这为我们双方节省了很多时间。
正如我上面提到的，我认为在一家大公司工作到L5(高年级)会更好。这并不是因为钱。在你的行业职业生涯开始时，这只是一种更有效的学习方式。
为什么在初创公司工作是个好主意/坏主意，有几个原因。这里我只谈钱。
初创企业则不同。你可以根据在那里工作的人数对他们进行分类。还有其他的分类模式，例如，基于它们的上一轮融资，但让我们停留在人数上。
每个阶段都有不同的文化，不同的流程，不同的人。通常，当一家初创公司从一个阶段进入另一个阶段时，公司的高管团队会发生变化。
“城市”类似于大型上市公司。“家庭”是硅谷车库式的刻板印象。
当我加入他们时，我的前两个职位是在大约50人(家庭/部落)的初创公司中。
让我们设想一下，你将从一家类似规模的初创公司获得一份工作机会。预计的赔偿范围是多少？
对于数据科学家/软件工程师来说，你的基本工资可能与上市公司相同，为120-15万美元。
股权部分更为棘手。最有可能的是，你将获得公司0.1%的股份，并有四年的归属时间表。
附注：一些初创公司，通常是那些粗略的初创公司，会告诉你：“N股，为期4年”。作为一个数字是没用的。你想知道公司的百分比，即知道M股总数中的N股。你需要问：“总共有多少股？”如果公司拒绝告诉你-这是一个大危险信号。
这家私人公司的股票之所以被称为“纸币”，是因为对于绝大多数初创公司来说，它的价值就像卫生纸一样。
记住，在一家大公司里，你每年会得到150-30万美元的补助。让我们在讨论中使用20万美元这个数字。
在一家上市公司，你得到了一份工作，你工作了4年，你没有得到晋升，但你没有被解雇，你得到了20万美元。
一家公司成为上市公司。示例：Lyft。只有在2019年的游行之后，员工拥有的股权才能转换为$$。
该公司被一家上市公司收购。重要！不是收购雇来的。而不是被另一家创业公司！如果你的初创公司被另一家初创公司收购，你将把一家公司的纸币兑换成另一家公司的纸币。
想象一下，奇迹发生了，你的公司上市了，你的0.1%得到了20万美元？让我们忘掉你在每轮融资中大约20%的稀释百分比。
20万美元/0.001美元=2,000,000美元(投资者不太可能允许该公司仅以2,000,000估值上市，但也让我们忘了这件事吧。)。
如果你在L4(中间)加入，工作了4年，工作得很好，没有被解雇，但又很糟糕，没有升职，那么从第一年开始，你将获得20万美元，4年后所有20万美元都将由你承担。
如果你作为第50个人加入一家初创公司，并努力工作，而且这家初创公司一直存活到首次公开募股(IPO)(可能性很小)，它将给你20万美元。但不是在你四年之后！该公司需要大约10年的时间才能上市。所以你要努力工作4年，再等6年才能拿到钱。
这笔交易听起来不太对劲。那么所有这些在早期加入成为百万富翁的人呢？
让我们来计算一下，以你的0.1%出价1000万美元的公司的IPO价格是多少？
1M/0.001=100亿美元。每年都有几家公司以这样或更高的估值上市。我猜测，2019年大约有10宗这样的大型IPO。
世界上有多少家初创企业？给你提供工作机会的初创公司成为这10家成功公司之一的可能性有多大？
我不是说这不会发生。在去年上市的一家公司工作了三年后，我的一个朋友获得了足够的资金，直到他的余生都不需要考虑钱的问题。我认识成千上万的人，他是唯一拿到这张彩票的人。
基本上，从财务角度来看，在初创公司中做一名低级别员工是很糟糕的。你将只依靠你的基本工资。你就不能用你的纸币付房租了。
操你的钱是一种花哨的方式，表示你有足够的钱，表明你在经济上完全独立。你可以对雇主或商业提议说“操你妈”，但这不会明显影响你的生活水平。
“去你妈的钱”不会让你快乐，但经济独立是一种理想的状态。
这个数字没有很好地定义，而且它取决于地理位置。在旧金山湾区，这一数字可能在1000万美元左右。
你在一家上市公司工作，你比平均水平要好，你是L6(员工)，你的总薪酬是50万美元/年。
税后，你每年有30万美元。你在房租、衣服、食物、啤酒和度假上花费了10万美元-剩下的20万美元你存入你的支票账户或投资于指数基金。
要达到“他妈的钱”阶段，你需要10M/200K=50年。
初创公司提供了另一条道路，这也是人们离开大公司转投小公司的原因之一。(通常，这不是主要原因。有很多比钱更重要的原因。)
当你到了L6，你就不再是低年级了。您拥有所谓的职业资本：良好的网络、技术、产品和营销技能。
你可以避免很多陷阱，选择一家成功机会更高的初创公司。
应届毕业生应聘初级/中级职位。FAANG(Facebook、Amazon、Apple、Netflix、Google)的L6经常被聘为数据科学主管、工程总监、首席数据科学家或类似的高级职位。这会对公司的业绩产生更显著的影响(你的技能和影响力越高，公司成功的机会就越大)，而且比0.1%的股权拨款要大得多。
在大型上市公司，你“操你妈的钱”的机会为零。当你拥有坚实的“职业资本”时，你加入的那些精明选择的初创公司，他们的薪酬要高得多。
作为一名应届毕业生，你不应该担心这件事。你应该瞄准大型科技公司，在面试更成熟的公司时，只把初创公司作为一种临时措施。
我想强调的是，钱不是万能的。我建议先在大型科技公司达到L5/L6级别，然后再考虑创业，这不是因为钱，而是因为学习曲线的形状。
我希望这篇博文澄清了游戏规则，以便更好地理解什么是“正常”，什么是“预期”。
note_en:
In spring 2020, graduates from the department who moved to Silicon Valley and worked in tech companies were invited to share the experience that they got in the industry with the current graduate students.
 The leadership of the department realized that the world had changed. In previous decades, graduates were looking to stay in academia. During graduate school, you were trained for the academic career: you need to do the research, publish papers, do experiments, teach, and apply for grants.
 The situation changed. Many graduates move to industry and go for the positions of software engineers, data scientists, and machine learning researchers. The sad truth is that my  alma mater was not preparing students for the transition at all.
 In this and following blog posts, I will try to share the information I wished I knew five years ago.
 Important: the target audience for this blog post are fresh graduates seeking their first job. It is less useful for people who have industry experience.
 Academia and industry are different bubbles. Way of thinking, professional deformation, what is essential in life, how projects are planned, what is acceptable, what is not, the “normal” level of political correctness, etc. The gap is enormous.
 Both bubbles complement each other really well. I love working with people that got an academic professional deformation while working on their PhDs and got industrial professional deformation working in the industry.
 In my  previous blog post, I talked about how academics can slightly improve the readability of the code that they write.
 Today I will talk about something that may be interesting even to those who do not write the code yet. This blog post will be about money.
  Note 1:  There will be a lot of $$$ numbers in the text. All of them before taxes! Taxes will eat a considerable part of your compensation. There are some ways to minimize the impact, but I will not talk about it.
 Note 2:  Some links in the post are referral links. I do not believe that people can make any money out of blogging and this is one of the ways to falsify my hypothesis :)
 When I was looking for my first job, I needed to answer the question: “What is the compensation I am looking for for my next role?” I did not know what to say. And I was not alone.
 One of my friends, who also graduated from our department, got an offer, HR (recruiter) asked: “How much do you want?”. He took his graduate student compensation of $25k. Multiplied by 3, added a bit extra, and told her: “80k”. The HR thought for a moment and answered: “Let’s make it $100k”. I think he could tell $130–150k and start negotiation from there. But he did not do his homework, he did not understand the market to the level that even the HR person was surprised.
 When you are a graduate student in the US, you are paid around $25k per year, which is fixed.
 When you work in the industry, the way that you compute your compensation is more involved.
 I believe that at the beginning of your industry career, it is better to work in a big tech company. In the first part, I will talk about compensation in public companies and later move to private companies, also known as startups.
  Geography. For the same work in Europe, you are paid much less than in the Bay area even if we talk about the same company, say Google.
 Your level. The more experienced you are, the better you are paid. In big companies, there is a structure called pay grades. Your skill is correlated with your grade, and each grade has a pay bracket. Higher bracket — higher pay.
 Company. The gap is not huge, but it may happen that even in the Bay area, Netflix will pay more than Amazon for the same work.
 Stock market. A big chunk of your compensation is equity. The stock of your company went up, you are paid more. It went down, it makes you less happy.
  Base pay: a fixed amount of $$$. It varies between $100k-200k per year for most of the people.
 RSU: equity that you can sell at the stock market. The more skilled you are, the more significant part of your compensation it is.
  Important to mention that the question: “ What was your offer?” differs from the question: “ How much do you make?” People confuse this a lot.
  At some point, companies introduce the formal structure for the employees’ skillset. It is called grades or levels. You will see it in all the big companies. Different companies have different notations for a grading system.  Levels.fyi provides a decent mapping from one notation to the other.
  The most widely accepted is the one used by Google, Facebook, Lyft, Uber, and others.
  Q: What about people that have big tech companies as their first industry full-time position? What level will they be considered?
 A: Fresh grads without a Ph.D. are considered for L3. Fresh grads with a Ph.D. are considered for L4.
 Q: Is it true that researchers are paid more than engineers?  A: No, it is not. Pay grades are precisely the same.
 Q: What if you have a Ph.D., have 100500 publications at the top conferences, and move to the industry, will they consider me for L5?
 A: If you get an offer, it will be an offer for L4. Publications may improve your chances of getting into the interviewing pipeline, but they will not affect the $$$ in the offer.
       You may get promoted. You go to the upper grade. Your base pay goes up, and you get an additional stock grant.
 You may get a bonus. Companies regularly give some money to the employees based on their performance. Typically once a year, in winter.
 If you perform well, you get a stock refresher. Typically once a year, in winter.
   An engineer joined company  A on July 7, 2019. She got $150k as a base pay and stock grant of $400k RSUs with a four-year vesting schedule. A four-year vesting schedule means that the employee will get only a quarter of this after the first year. RSUs are not measured in $$$. They are measured in the units of equity. On July 7, 2019, the stock market price was $60 per share, and she got a stock grant of 6666 shares.
 She expected to get for the next year of work $250k = $150k +($400k / 4).
 July 7, 2020, if she sells RSUs with the current stock price of $30 she will get:
 $150k + ($400k / 4 / $60 (last year price)) * $30 (this year price) = $200k
    Software Engineer accepted an offer to company  B: base $150k and a four-year grant for $400k RSUs.
  $150k + ($400k / 4 / $230 (last year price) * $1389 (this year price)) = $754k
  Was it obvious the year ago that it will turn out that way? Not really. Do we know what will happen in the future? No, we do not. Timing stock markets is a bad idea.
 Q: I joined the company as a fresh grad with Ph.D. How long will it take to get promoted to L5?
 A: It depends. You are not entitled to a promotion. You may get promoted, or may not. If you are not promoted from L4 to L5 in 3 years, you need to consider another role or another company. Hopefully, you will be able to realize your potential there.
 Q: Compensation numbers for L6 look attractive. How long does this take to get promoted from L5 to L6?
 A: L5 is the terminal level. 90% of the employees never get above it. Let’s say best case scenario — another 3 years. More realistic — it will never happen.
  A: As a first step, you need to get the offer. The interview process is very competitive, and no one likes to hire fresh grads. No one in the world knows how to set up the interview process well. Weak candidates get offers, strong candidates get rejected. Hiring strong talent at scale is a very complex problem. It is hard to estimate how productive the candidate will be in a few hours that the team spends talking with him. The strength of the candidate and his ability to pass interviews are not perfectly aligned. For fresh grads, this is even more random than for seniors.
 Internships help to mitigate this problem for people from academia. During the internship, the candidate works on something close to the problem that the company is facing, and he does it in collaboration with full-time employees. As a result of the internship, the person may get an offer for a full-time position. The vast majority of the offers for fresh grads in big tech companies are through internships.
 If you have more than a year till graduation, you need to focus on getting internships. It will help with your skills and your career. As a bonus, it will also help you financially. In graduate school, you are paid around $2000 / month, and as an intern, this number will be around $9000 / month.
 Q: What will matter the most for the $$$ in the offer, my great publications, or my internships.  A: Internships.
 Q: If I will get an offer. How can it look?  A: Just check  level.fyi for L4 and look for the lower part of the range.
  I am not an expert. Every time I accepted an offer, I felt that the recruiter manipulated me to take something lower than I could get.
  The book about negotiation that I recommend to everyone, even if you are far away from the job search process:  E  verything Is Negotiable: How to Get the Best Deal Every Time .
  The most important is to get offers from many different companies. It will make your position stronger and help companies understand your market value, measured by an external source.
 Do not say the number first. The company should do it. Negotiation is based on the asymmetry of the information. It is not your obligation to share anything unless you want it.
  How much do you make now? This is illegal in California, but recruiters still do it.
   This is my favorite:  “What is the minimum number that will make you accept the offer?”
 There are exceptions to all these rules. When people contact me with a proposal to interview for a position that they have, I clearly state my expectations about the compensation. They disappear, and this saves both of us a lot of time.
  As I mentioned above, I believe that it is better to work in a big company until you reach L5 (senior). And it is not because of the money. At the beginning of your industry career, it is just a more efficient way to learn.
 There are several reasons why working in a startup is a good/bad idea. Here I will talk only about money.
 Startups are different. You may classify them by the number of people that work there. There are other classification schemas, for example, based on their last funding round, but let’s stay with the number of people.
   Every stage has a different culture, processes, people. Pretty often, the company’s executive team is changed when a startup goes from one stage to the other.
 “The city ” is similar to big public companies. “Family” is a stereotypical Silicon Valley garage-type endeavor.
 My first two positions were in the startups of around 50 people (family/tribe) when I joined them.
 Let’s imagine that you will get an offer from a similar size startup. What is the expected compensation range?
 For a Data Scientist / Software Engineer, you can expect the same base pay as you would have in a public company of $120–150k.
 The equity component is trickier. Most likely, you will be offered a grant of 0.1% of the company with a four-year vesting schedule.
 Side note:  Some startups, typically those that are sketchy, will tell you: “N shares for 4 years”. N — as a number is useless. You want the percent of the company, i.e. to know N out of total M shares. You need to ask: “What is the total number of shares?”. If the company refuses to tell you — this is a big red flag.
 The private company’s stock is called “paper money” because, for the vast majority of the startups, it will be as valuable as toilet paper.
  Remember, in a big company, you will get a grant for $150–300k / year. Let’s use the number of $200k in our discussion.
 In a public company, you get an offer, you work for 4 years, you do no get promoted, but you do not get fired, and you get your $200k.
   A company becomes public. Example: Lyft. Only after a march of 2019, the equity that employees had could be converted into $$$.
 The company gets acquired by a public company.  Important! Not acquihired. And not by another startup! If your startup is acquired by another startup, you will convert paper money from one company to the other’s paper money.
  Imagine that the miracle happened, and your company became public, you got $200k for your 0.1%? And let’s forget about ~20% dilution of your percentage on every financing round.
 $200k / 0.001 = $200 million (It is unlikely that investors will allow the company to get public for only 200 million valuations, but let’s also forget about it.)
 If you joined at L4 (middle) and worked for 4 years so good that you were not fired but so bad that you were not promoted, you will get $200k, starting from the first year, and all $200k will be on your account in 4 years.
 If you joined a startup as a 50th person and worked hard, and that startup survived till IPO (very unlikely), it will give you $200k. But not after your four years! It takes around 10 years for the company to become public. Hence your work hard for 4 years and wait for another 6 years to get your money.
 This deal does not sound right. What about all these people that became millionaires, joining at early stages?
 Let’s calculate the IPO price for the company that gave $10,000,000 for your 0.1%?
 1m / 0.001 = $10 billion. Every year a few companies become public with this or higher valuation. I would guess that in 2019 there were around 10 such big IPOs.
 How many startups are there in the world? What are the chances that the startup that gave you an offer will be one of these successful 10?
 I am not saying that this does not happen. After three years of work in a company that became public last year, one of my friends got enough capital that he does not need to think about money until the rest of his life. I know thousands of people, and he is the only one who got this lottery ticket.
 Basically, on average, from a financial perspective, being a low-level employee in a startup sucks. You will rely only on your base pay. And you will not be able to use your paper money to pay rent.
   Fuck you money is a fancy way of saying enough  money that  you’re completely financially independent. You can say “ fuck you” to an employer or a business proposition, and it won’t affect your living standards noticeably.
  “Fuck you money” will not make you happy, but financial independence is a desirable state.
 This number is not well defined, and it depends on geography. In the Bay area, it is probably around $10,000,000.
 You work at a public company, you are better than average, you are L6 (Staff), and your total compensation is $500k / year.
 After taxes, you have $300k / year. You spend $100k on rent, clothes, food, beer, and vacations — the rest of $200k you put on your checking account or invest in index funds.
 To get to the “Fuck you Money” stage, you need 10m / 200k = 50 years.
  Startups offer another path, which is one of the reasons why people leave big companies for smaller ones. (Typically, it is not the main reason. There are plenty of reasons that are more important than money.)
 When you get to L6, you are not junior anymore. You have what is called a career capital: good networking, technical, product, and marketing skills.
  You can avoid many pitfalls and choose a startup that has a higher chance of success.
 Fresh grads go for junior/middle positions. L6 from  FAANG (Facebook, Amazon, Apple, Netflix, Google) are often hired for Head of Data Science, Director of Engineering, Chief Data Scientist, or similar high-level positions. This leads to a more significant impact on the company’s performance (more skilled and impactful you are, more chances for the company’s success) and much larger than a 0.1% equity grant.
 In the big public company, your chances for “Fuck you money” are zero. In the wisely chosen startup that you join when you have a solid “career capital,” they are much higher.
 As a fresh graduate, you should not worry about it. You should aim for big tech companies and go for startups only as a temporary measure while interviewing for more mature ones.
 I want to emphasize that money is not everything. I recommend getting to the L5/L6 level in the big tech company first and consider going for the startups later, not because of the money, but because of the shape of the learning curve.
 I hope this blog post clarified the rules of the game to have a better understanding of what is “normal” and what is “expected.”
title: 应届毕业生在硅谷的薪酬
title_en: Fresh Grad’s Compensation in Silicon Valley

Statifier是一个用于创建可移植的、自包含的Linux可执行文件的工具。统计器从动态链接的可执行文件及其所有库创建一个文件。此文件可以复制并在另一台计算机上运行，而无需拖动它的所有库。动态链接的可执行文件比静态链接的可执行文件小。另一方面，动态链接的可执行文件使用以PIC(与位置无关的代码)编译的共享库，这比普通的要慢。由统计器(伪静态)创建的文件结合了两者的缺点：那么，为什么要使用它呢？好的，伪静态文件也有一些优点：但是所有这些都可以用普通的静态文件来获得。那么，为什么要使用统计器和巨型伪静态可执行文件而不是静态可执行文件呢？要保留内存布局-静态链接将更改它，并可能唤醒隐藏的错误。
支持的平台静态可执行文件并不总是在具有堆栈或VDSO随机化的系统上工作。不幸的是，我还没有找到解决这个问题的方法。还有另一种解决方案：貂皮。貂皮，不同于统计器，不受堆叠/VDSO随机化的影响。此处比较的是稳定器和Ermine特征。
有用的(我希望)另外还有一个rrp_statifypackage，这是一个用于rpmrebuild的插件，它允许统计包中的所有可执行文件。为了使统计化的可执行文件更小，可以按照Andrew Shewaker的建议尝试使用Libraryopt。如果您有建议/想法/错误报告，您可以将其发送到邮件列表或论坛，如果对您有效，我很想知道您的平台是什么：请给我留言
note_en:
Statifier is a tool for creating portable, self-containing Linux executables. Statifier create from dynamically linked executablesand all it's libraries one file. This file can be copied and run on another machinewithout need to drag all it's libraries. Dynamically linked executables are smaller then statically linked. From the other side dynamically linked executables use shared libraries compiled in PIC (position independend code) which is slower than "normal" one. Files, created by statifier ("pseudo-static")combine disadvantages of both of them:  So, why one will use it ? Ok, "pseudo-static" files have some advantages too:  But all these one can got with usual static files. So, why use statifier and huge "pseudo-static" executables instead of static ?   to preserve memory layout - static link will change it and may "wake-up" hidden bugs
 Supported platforms  Statified executables  not always work on systems with stack or VDSO randomization. Unfortunatelly I yet found a way to fix this. There is another solution:  Ermine.     Ermine, unlike statifier not affected by stack/VDSO randomization.     Statifier and Ermine features compared  here
  Usefull (I hope) additional There is a  rrp_statifypackage, a plugin for  rpmrebuildwhich allow statify all executables in the package. In order to make statified executables smaller one can try to use Libraryopt as suggested by Andrew Shewmaker. If you have suggestions/ideas/bug report you can send it to mailing list or  forums If it work for you I am curious to know what is your platform: Please drop me a  message
title: ELF统计器，自包含动态链接的可执行文件
title_en: ELF Statifier, self-contained dynamically linked executables

远程工作，曾经被认为是一种新鲜事物或奇怪的实验，已经成为新冠肺炎世界中许多企业的必需品。这挑战了远程工作环境阻碍员工工作效率的普遍假设。随着员工迅速转向在家工作，各行各业的高管都对生产率保持稳定甚至提高感到惊讶，其中许多人兼任家庭学校教师。然而，不知何故，公司从中得出的教训并不是他们应该全程远程办公，而是他们应该混合办公：将远程工作和代管工作结合起来。无论你如何处理，混合远程都是很困难的，最终，试图两者兼而有之的公司要么全力以赴地使用远程，要么回到以办公室为基础的模式。
Sid Sijbrdij是GitLab的联合创始人和首席执行官，GitLab是一个帮助团队在软件开发和项目管理方面进行协作的DevOps平台。该公司已经从高盛(Goldman Sachs)、ICONIQ Capital、科斯拉风险投资公司(Khosla Ventures)、谷歌风险投资公司(Google Ventures)和奥古斯特资本公司等公司筹集了超过4.26亿美元。
混合模式创造了两种完全不同的员工管理体验。尽管最近在远程工作方面取得了成功，但雇主正在重新向一些员工开放办公室，以鼓励社会纽带，加强文化，并加强业务协作。这些重新开业背后的假设是，一些关键的事情不能在办公室之外同样有效地完成。在办公室建立业务的领导者指望共享空间充当文化的粘合剂，并作为沟通系统和流程低效的权宜之计。虽然基于办公室的模式在历史上被证明对许多公司来说是成功的，但对于致力于支持远程员工的公司来说，它将带来巨大的挑战。如果办公室是“粘合剂”，而流程和系统不适合远程员工，远程团队成员将感觉不到被包括在内，并将面临持续的沟通障碍。这将使他们的表现更难达到与办公室同龄人相同的水平。
无论何时，只要有总部，即领导层所在的物理共处空间，总会有两种沟通方式。为了让每个人处于同一水平，一家公司需要领导层离开共享办公室，这样没有一个单独的物理位置比另一个地方拥有更多的权力。然而，实际上，混合远程公司的大多数领导层将继续在总部工作，将默认的远程工作方式从远程优先降至远程允许。在这种情况下，远程员工不会因为在办公室外工作而受到惩罚，也不会主动融入公司架构。对于团队成员来说，这将是一个特别令人沮丧的结果，因为他们的组织向他们兜售他们可以选择远程工作作为福利的想法。员工们很快就会发现，公司没有从奖励出勤的旧方式(将办公室里的“被人看到”等同于出色的员工)转变为奖励产出的新方式(将取得的成果等同于出色的员工)。最终，远程员工会发现他们得不到同等的晋升，因为他们不那么显眼，而且有生产力的远程员工将离开，前往投资于其远程团队成员的全远程公司。
但这并不一定要以这种方式发生。远程员工(混合员工或完全远程员工)的成功需要有运营意向。毫无疑问，固守使基于办公室的模型成功的系统和流程将注定任何远程模型都会失败。
作为GitLab的联合创始人和首席执行官，我很早就发现，不需要每个人都在同一座大楼里就能取得成果，于是我主动创建了一家全远程公司。我们的高管、经理和个人贡献者都在远程工作。我们甚至没有总部。这避免了必须迎合现场和非现场员工的复杂性。它还促进了对我们独特的运营和迭代方式的共同承诺，以随着时间的推移而改进。
在家工作永远不会是每个人的最佳解决方案，“远程”并不总是意味着“家”。如果团队成员想要在办公室工作，因为他们的家不利于工作，他们被室友或家人分心，他们想要共同工作空间的社交互动，或者他们只是觉得离家在外更有效率，我们会支付空间费用。然而，我们注意到，一些刚开始喜欢办公室的人在最初的几个月里会过渡到在家工作。
我们还关心并鼓励非正式的数字交流，如咖啡聊天，甚至全远程选秀节目。这并不是说我们没有认识到面对面交流的重要性：在大流行之前，我们每年都会组织一次面对面的全员工活动，并鼓励居住在同一地区的团队成员在当地会面。我们
主动将您的组织设置为远程，这超出了您坐的位置和您与其他人见面的频率。这就是为什么我们不让来自不同领域的员工向同一位经理汇报，即使我们的大部分工作是跨领域的。相反，我们要求每个人都是一个人的经理，可以在没有项目经理的情况下自组织和异步工作。
虽然领导一家全远程公司将需要许多经理重新思考和重新制定他们的业务运营方式，但全远程是可能的，并将带来更强的危机韧性、更高的效率和获得以前遥不可及的人才的机会。在大城市地区之外有如此多的人才，这些人才将突然能够在世界领先的公司竞争高薪和有回报的工作。这将有助于在世界各地更平均地分配收入(即使不平等仍将加剧)。
在办公室关闭后的这段时间里，一些公司已经取消了租约，打算全程远程办公。另一方面，许多公司致力于减少他们在办公室的存在，而不是消除它，并计划走向混合远程。那些混血的人，如果不是有意进行系统性的改变，把每个员工都当作远程员工对待(无论他们在不在办公室)，他们最有效率的远程员工将会离开。然后，混合型公司会将工作效率低下归咎于偏远地区，而不是实际原因：管理两种截然不同的员工体验是一项非常艰巨的任务。这些公司将把远程工作视为一种新的实验，将运营困难归咎于远程工作，并将剩余的远程工作人员拉回办公室。

note_en:
Remote work, once considered a novelty or an odd experiment, has become a necessity for many businesses in the  Covid-19 world. This has challenged the common assumption that remote work environments hinder employee productivity. Executives across industries have marveled at productivity remaining consistent or even increasing as employees rapidly shifted to working from home with many of them doing double duty as home-school teachers. Yet, somehow, the lesson that companies have deduced from this isn’t that they should go all-remote, but instead that they should go hybrid: combining remote and co-located work. No matter how you approach it, hybrid-remote is hard, and in the end, companies that attempt to do both will either go all-in with remote or go back to being office-based.
 Sid Sijbrandij is the co-founder and CEO of GitLab, which is a DevOps platform that helps teams collaborate on software development and project management. The company has raised over $426 million from Goldman Sachs, ICONIQ Capital, Khosla Ventures, Google Ventures, and August Capital, among others.
 Hybrid creates two fundamentally different employee experiences to manage. Despite recent successes with remote work, employers are reopening offices to some of their employees to encourage social bonding, reinforce culture, and increase business collaboration. The assumption underlying these reopenings is that some critical things can’t be done as effectively outside of the office. Leaders who built their businesses in offices counted on shared space to act as a glue for culture and as a stopgap for inefficiencies in communication systems and processes. While the office-based model has historically proven to be successful for many companies, it will provide significant challenges for companies committed to also supporting a remote workforce. If an office is the “glue”, and processes and systems don’t adapt for a remote workforce, remote team members will not feel included and will face constant communication barriers. This will make it harder for them to perform at the same level as their in-office peers.
  And whenever there is a head-office, a physical co-located space where leadership resides, there will always be two ways of communicating. To get everyone on the same level, a company would need leadership to leave the shared office so no single physical place holds more power than another. Realistically, however, most leadership in hybrid-remote firms will keep working from the head-office, degrading the default way of working from 'remote-first' to 'remote-allowed,’ where remote employees are not penalized for working outside the office, but are also not proactively integrated into the fabric of the company. This will be a particularly frustrating result for team members whose organizations sold them on the idea that they could opt-in to remote work as a perk. Employees will quickly discover that the company didn't make the shift from the old ways of rewarding attendance (equating ‘being seen’ in an office with being a great worker) to a new way of rewarding output (equating achieved results with being a great worker). Eventually, remote workers will find that they are not getting promoted at an equal rate because they are less visible and the productive remote employees will leave for all-remote companies that invest in their remote team members.
 But it doesn’t have to happen that way. Success with a remote workforce, hybrid or fully remote, requires operational intentionality. Unquestioningly sticking to systems and processes that made an office-based model successful will doom any remote model to fail.
  As a co-founder and the CEO of GitLab, I proactively built an all-remote company after discovering early-on that you don’t need everyone in the same building to achieve results. Our executives, managers, and individual contributors all work remote. We don’t even have an HQ. This avoids the complexities of having to cater to onsite and offsite employees. It also fosters a shared commitment to our unique way of operating and iterating to improve over time.
  Working from home will never be the best solution for everyone and “remote” doesn’t always mean “home.” If a team member wants to work in an office because their home isn’t conducive to work, they’re distracted by roommates or family members, they want the social interaction of a co-working space, or they simply feel more productive away from home, we'll pay for the space. We have noticed, however, that some people who prefer an office in the beginning transition to work-from-home during their first months.
 We also care about and  encourage informal digital communication such as coffee chats and even all-remote talent shows. This isn’t to say we don’t recognize the importance of in-person face-time: Pre-pandemic, we organized an in-person all-employee event every year and encouraged local meet-ups amongst team members who live in the same region. We also offered visiting grants, which provided company subsidized travel to visit team members in another location anywhere in the world, and we look forward to bringing them back.
  Proactively setting up your organization for remote goes beyond where you sit and how often you see other people. That's why we don't have employees from different disciplines report to the same manager even though much of our work is multi-disciplinary. Instead, we require each individual to be a 'manager of one' who can self-organize and work asynchronously without a project manager.
 While leading an all-remote company will require many managers to rethink and rework how they run their businesses, all-remote is possible and will lead to greater resilience to crises, increased efficiency and access to talent that was previously out of reach. There is so much talent outside of major metropolitan areas, and this talent will suddenly be able to compete for high paying and rewarding jobs at the world's leading companies. This will help spread income a bit more equally around the world (even if inequality will still rise).
 In the time since offices shut down, some companies have already canceled their leases with the intent to go all-remote. On the other hand, many companies are intent on reducing their in-office presence, rather than eliminating it, and plan to go hybrid-remote. Those who do hybrid, if not intentional about making systemic changes and treating every employee as if they are remote (whether in-office or not), will see their most effective remote people leave. The hybrid companies will then blame the lack of productivity on remote instead of the actual cause: managing two distinct employee experiences is a very arduous task. These companies will write off remote work as a novel experiment, blame it for operational difficulties, and pull remaining remote workers back into the office.

协议手册：医疗保健远程治疗应用程序是越来越多的服务之一，这些服务试图重新发明在大流行期间提供治疗的方式。
过去几个月的事实证明，Talkspace在很多事情上都是正确的。视频就是其中之一：当Talkspace在2012年推出时，它的主要设计目标是通过视频聊天来进行治疗，但该公司很快意识到，对人们来说真正吸引人的是能够与他们的治疗师发短信。这对Talkspace来说没有问题-Talkspace的首席执行官奥伦·弗兰克(Oren Frank)说，它的目标只是让人们更容易获得治疗，无论他们想要什么。短信成为了Talkspace的首要任务，并一直保持了多年。
不过，最近，视频又卷土重来了。这要归咎于大流行，以及每个人突然习惯于整天盯着缩放或团队窗口的事实。弗兰克说：“我想我们都因为这种情况被放大了，所以对视频会议的需求更高了。这对Talkspace来说也没问题。”它提供视频、音频和文本，供客户和治疗师随心所欲地使用。每一种患者和治疗师的关系都是不同的，这正是我们的想法。
对于Talkspace来说，更紧迫的变化是需要这样东西的人的绝对数量。多亏了新冠肺炎，许多已经接受治疗的人被迫寻找新的方式来治疗。这场流行病还导致了精神健康问题的激增：凯撒家庭基金会(Kaiser Family Foundation)的一项民意调查发现，45%的美国成年人表示，由于对病毒的担忧和压力，他们的心理健康受到了负面影响。美国疾病控制与预防中心(CDC)的一项调查发现，一年前报告出现焦虑或抑郁症状的人数几乎增加了两倍。再加上史无前例的失业率，对种族不公正的斗争，以及社交媒体引发的焦虑情绪的持续上升，治疗对更多的人来说从未像现在这样重要。
持续的心理健康危机迫使Talkspace和其他虚拟护理服务，如BetterHealth和MDLive，重新考虑它们是如何运作的，以及它们是否可以扩大规模。将治疗从每周在某处某处一小时的治疗转变为基于文本的持续对话当然会向更多的人开放，但这仍然是一个一对一的过程，需要时间和个性化的护理。让它变得更便宜--Talkspace的起步价是每周65美元，如果算上每周的现场治疗，就会涨到99美元，而面对面的每周治疗的费用几乎是这个数字的两倍--吸引了更多的人，但许多人仍然负担不起。因此，Talkspace和其他公司正在与保险公司和监管机构合作，试验人工智能工具和社交媒体战略，并总体上试图找到一切可能的方式来扩大他们的业务。(Talkspace目前还没有盈利。)。这既是因为现在对这些应用程序来说是一个前所未有的商机，其中许多应用程序已经筹集了数百万美元的风险投资，也因为它只是觉得这是应该做的事情。
Talkspace是虚拟治疗领域最大的玩家。根据Crunchbase的数据，它已经筹集了1.067亿美元的资金，并表示它拥有100多万用户和数千名治疗师。它实际上并没有雇佣治疗师，而是运营着一个类似于优步(Uber)的市场，中间是Talkspace，让一切保持运转，并从中分一杯羹。正如司机们感受到拼车公司的虐待一样，治疗师们表示，一个平台承诺给他们更多的灵活性和金钱，但往往没有兑现，他们觉得自己的薪酬过低，工作过度。由于对何时以及如何应对有严格的要求，谈话空间治疗师的工资是按字支付的，而且通常每小时的收入比他们自己要少得多。近年来，该公司花了很多时间试图找出一种方法，让这个系统为每个人服务。而且它还在努力解决这个问题。
但基本的想法-虚拟治疗可以是有效的治疗-是有效的。一些研究表明，它和面对面治疗一样有效，而另一些人则说，在最坏的情况下，它的成效略低，但所有人都同意虚拟治疗是有效的。随着世界各地的卫生机构转向在人们的家中进行更多的护理，而不是在医院和医生的办公室进行更少的护理，Talkspace的方法似乎可能是其中的一部分。
就像远程工作一样，远程治疗是一种长期的趋势，因为大流行突然进入了超光速状态。而且科技正在渗透到这个过程的每一个部分。牛津大学焦虑症和创伤中心受COVID的启发，为远程治疗师提供的指导方针包括使用谷歌街景为创伤后应激障碍患者访问创伤性场所。它还就如何让视频变得更可口提供了建议，比如让患者隐藏他们自己的视频视图。它还鼓励分享你的屏幕，和病人一起写故事。
现在有两种类型的患者：一种是习惯于面对面治疗并正在转向远程治疗的患者，另一种是第一次接受治疗的患者。在后一种情况下，Talkspace首席医疗官Neil Leibowitz告诉礼宾，衡量成功的一个关键标准是人们与他们的治疗师建立联系的速度有多快。你喜欢它们吗？莱博维茨说，在第一天，这比他们的治疗能力更重要。随着时间的推移，他们的治疗能力变得非常重要，但如果你不喜欢他们，你就再也回不去了。研究表明，人们可以在看不到他们的脸或听到他们的声音的情况下与治疗师建立联系。
长期以来，Talkspace和其他虚拟治疗服务面临的一个挑战是，保险公司并不真正知道如何看待它们。莱博维茨说：我总是说远程医疗已经存在了一个多世纪。医生们在电话里交谈。我们只是从来没有想过如何支付费用。但现在治疗师正在试验这项技术，患者正在寻找像Talkspace这样的服务，保险提供商也开始接受这种服务。
信诺负责行为和医疗解决方案的董事总经理伊娃·博登(Eva Borden)说，她关注Talkspace已经有一段时间了。几年前，信诺开始向患者报销与他们的面对面治疗师进行的虚拟会议。但在5月份，它将Talkspace添加为临床提供商。对于博登来说，这也是关于获得医疗服务的问题。她说，有了Talkspace，就不再有那么多关于时钟何时告诉你需要帮助的问题了。当你需要它的时候，它就在那里。这对我们来说真的很重要。许多保险公司仍然不承保Talkspace，但莱博维茨表示，这种情况正在迅速改变。
监管可能是Talkspace增长的更大障碍。州法规通常要求治疗师在他们执业的州获得执照，这对于实体环境中的人来说很简单，但当一个治疗师理论上可以在世界各地拥有虚拟客户时，这就很复杂了。在过去，监管机构甚至建议，在州外旅行的患者可能无法从他们通常的治疗师那里获得治疗。这种情况已经改变了一段时间，这要归功于像心理学管辖范围内契约(简称PSYPACT)这样的规则，PSYPACT是一个由多个心理学委员会成立的小组，目的是创建州际系统来实践远程治疗。COVID还促使各州暂时放松限制。但是改变发生得很慢。
随着这些规定的改变，博登说，她对远程治疗感到兴奋的一个原因是，它可以帮助患者找到合适的治疗师，无论他们在哪里。她说，行为健康提供者短缺，特别是在农村地区。她说，帮助人们找到理解他们和他们问题的人，比仅仅走进最近的门要有用得多。(Talkspace最近一直在努力帮助黑人患者找到更了解他们背景的治疗师。)。
博登说，未来不只是虚拟的，当大流行结束时，世界也不会回到仅仅面对面的互动。她说：我的目标是提供选择权，让人们以他们想要的方式得到护理。不管病人需要什么，不管他们需要什么。
不过，所有这些模式和选项仍然有一个共同点，那就是它们都是一对一的体验--治疗师通常有很多客户，但他们一次只与一个客户合作。当大流行来袭时，Talkspace向一线工作人员捐赠了几个小时的治疗时间，弗兰克说，他正在寻找向需要的人提供更多免费服务的方法。但是，因为每个新客户都需要治疗师的时间和注意力，所以这种方法就是无法达到规模。
因此，Talkspace回到了它的基本目标：接入。还有什么比在这个地球上最受欢迎的社交平台上接触更多人的方式呢？今年3月，Talkspace建立了一些Facebook群组。最受欢迎的是管理冠状病毒焦虑的免费公共支持小组，有近3000名成员。他们经常由Talkspace治疗师主持，他们为他们花费时间主持小组活动而获得报酬，但他们并不是在接受治疗。每组顶部的长篇免责声明非常清楚地表明了这一点。相反，它们是一个让更多的人相互交谈的地方，有时还可以与专业人士交谈。
这些团体里挤满了寻找信息或查看信息的人，以确保他们并不是唯一一个在这些疯狂的时期完全无法工作、无法思考或完全无法集中精力的人。除了极少数例外，这些评论都是积极的、有益的和友好的。虽然通话空间治疗师不是付钱去那里的，但他们经常会贴出提示并提出问题。
Talkspace的博客、Instagram和Twitter页面也起到了类似的作用。Talkspace在Instagram上发布快速减压的帖子，人们开始在Instagram上加入进来；它发布创伤后应激障碍的迹象，人们评论说，这就是我。该公司在Instagram上托管了Q&amp；AS，推出了一系列名为Talkspace治疗师日记的节目，每月拥有数百万博客读者。
所有这些都很好，但如果Talkspace想要达到真正的规模，让每个人都能获得虚拟治疗，那么只有一条路要走：将人类治疗师完全从等式中剔除，取而代之的是人工智能聊天机器人。治疗机器人在很大程度上正在成为一种东西。弗兰克对整个想法不屑一顾。谈论人工智能与治疗或许多其他形式的医疗保健有关的人，他说，我认为他们50年来讨论这个问题还为时过早--如果真的有的话。
弗兰克说，Talkspace已经围绕人工智能进行了一些测试和尝试了一些事情，但目标是帮助治疗师，而不是取代他们。弗兰克希望利用数据帮助培训治疗师何时使用视频而不是音频，或者帮助精神病学家找出开哪些药以及什么时候开。Talkspace还有一种风险算法，它用来标记特别令人担忧的时刻或消息，这样治疗师就可以更快、更正确地做出反应。
人工智能可以是一种让更多人获得更多帮助的方式，但Talkspace主要专注于如何让治疗师的事情变得更容易，他们中的许多人也仍在研究如何在这个新的多平台治疗空间中运作。弗兰克说：我们的战略和我们的信念是，在可预见的未来，人类是一种非常、非常优越的技术。但是人类和鳞片并不总是混为一谈。Talkspace最近放宽了其保证响应时间指南，该指南告诉治疗师他们需要以多快的速度回复患者的信息，以便让治疗师有更多时间而不会陷入麻烦。
对Talkspace的需求，以及整个虚拟治疗行业的需求，都在持续增长。Leibowitz说，跟踪治疗需求的一个捷径是跟踪经济-随着美国陷入衰退，这种需求继续上升。弗兰克说，在Talkspace内部，目前的系统感觉很管用。或者，至少感觉像是Talkspace能做的最多。网络压力很大，我们的客户服务压力很大，他说。
最终，似乎每个人都希望实体治疗能为那些需要的人回来。我的整个目标是让每个人都得到他们需要的护理，不管是什么，信诺的博登说。但由于所有这些实体店都关闭了，过去几个月一直是对Talkspace、BetterHealth和其他技术前沿治疗公司想要的一切的压力测试-那种感觉像是未来的灵活、多平台的治疗。莱博维茨说，考虑到所有因素，我们的表现相当不错，我非常自豪我们能够帮助人们。

note_en:
Protocol Manual: Health Care   The teletherapy app is one of a growing number of services trying to reinvent the way therapy is delivered in the middle of a pandemic.
     The last few months have proven Talkspace right about a lot of things. Video, for one: When Talkspace launched in 2012, it was primarily designed to be a tool for therapy by video chat, but the company realized quickly that what was really clicking for people was being able to text with their therapist. That was fine with Talkspace — its goal, CEO Oren Frank said, was simply to make therapy more accessible to people, however they wanted it. Messaging became Talkspace's primary thing and remained so for years.
  Recently, though, video's making a comeback. Blame the pandemic, and the fact that everyone's suddenly used to staring into a Zoom or Teams window all day. "I think we've all been Zoomified because of the situation," Frank said, "so there's been higher demand for video sessions." That's fine with Talkspace, too. It offers video, audio and text, for clients and therapists to use however they'd like. Every patient-therapist relationship is different, which is exactly the idea.
  The more pressing change for Talkspace is the sheer number of people who need something like it. Thanks to COVID-19, many people who already go to therapy have been forced to find new ways to do so. The pandemic has also caused a spike in mental health issues: a  Kaiser Family Foundation poll found that 45% of adults in the United States said "their mental health has been negatively impacted due to worry and stress over the virus." A CDC survey found that the number of people reporting symptoms of anxiety or depression is nearly triple a year ago. Combine that with unprecedented levels of unemployment, the fight over racial injustice and the ongoing rise in social media-induced anxiety, and therapy's never been more essential to more people.
 The ongoing mental-health crisis has forced Talkspace and other virtual-care services, like BetterHealth and MDLive, to rethink how they work and whether they can scale. Turning therapy from a weekly hour in a building somewhere to an ongoing text-based conversation certainly opens it up to more people, but it's still a one-on-one process that requires time and individualized care. Making it cheaper — Talkspace starts at $65 a week and goes up to $99 when you include a weekly live session, while in-person weekly therapy sessions can cost nearly double that — brings in more people, but many still can't afford it. So Talkspace and others are working with insurance companies and regulators, experimenting with AI tools and social media strategies, and generally trying to find every possible way to scale their business. (Talkspace isn't currently profitable.) Both because now is an unprecedented business opportunity for these apps, many of which have raised millions in venture capital, and because it simply feels like the right thing to do.
  Talkspace is the largest player in the virtual-therapy space. It's raised $106.7 million in funding, according to Crunchbase, and says it has more than 1 million users and thousands of therapists on board. It doesn't actually employ therapists, but rather runs a marketplace similar to Uber, with Talkspace in the middle keeping everything running and taking a cut. And just as drivers have felt mistreated by ride-sharing companies, therapists have said they feel  underpaid and overworked by a platform that promises them more flexibility and money but often doesn't follow through. Talkspace therapists are paid by the word, given strict requirements about when and how to respond, and typically make far less per hour than they would on their own. The company has spent much time in recent years trying to figure out a way to make the system work for everyone. And it's still working on it.
 But the fundamental idea — that virtual therapy can be effective therapy — works. Some  studies show that it's  just as effective as in-person treatment, whereas  others say it's at worst slightly less productive, but all agree that virtual therapy works. As health agencies around the world shift to doing more care in people's homes and less in hospitals and doctor's offices, Talkspace's methods seem likely to be part of it.
  Like remote work, remote therapy is a longstanding trend suddenly thrown into hyperdrive by the pandemic. And tech is seeping into every part of the process. The Oxford University Centre for Anxiety Disorders and Trauma's  COVID-inspired guidelines for remote therapists include using Google Street View to visit traumatic places for patients with PTSD. It also offers recommendations on how to make video more palatable, like having patients hide their view of their own video. And it encourages sharing your screen and writing a narrative together with patients.
 There are two types of patients right now: those who are used to in-person therapy and are switching to remote, and those who are receiving care for the first time. In the latter case, one key measure of success, Talkspace Chief Medical Officer Neil Leibowitz told Protocol, is how quickly people bond with their therapist. "Do you like them? On day one, that's more important than their ability to treat," Leibowitz said. "Over time, their ability to treat becomes very important, but if you don't like them, you never go back."  Studies have shown that people can bond with therapists without ever seeing their face or hearing their voice.
 One challenge for Talkspace and other virtual therapy services has long been that insurance companies didn't really know what to make of them. "I always say telehealth has been around for over a century," Leibowitz said. "Doctors talked on the telephone. We just never figured out how to pay for it." But now that therapists are experimenting with the tech, and patients are finding services like Talkspace, insurance providers are coming around.
  Eva Borden, Cigna's managing director for behavioral and medical solutions, said she's been watching Talkspace for a while. Cigna began reimbursing patients for virtual sessions with their otherwise in-person therapists a couple of years ago. But in May, it added Talkspace as a clinical provider. For Borden, too, it's about access to care. With Talkspace, she said, "it stops being so much about when the clock told you you needed to get help. It's there when you need it. That was a really major part for us." Many insurers still don't cover Talkspace, but Leibowitz said that's changing quickly.
 Regulation may be an even bigger hurdle for Talkspace's growth. State regulations typically require that a therapist be licensed in the state in which they practice, which is simple for someone in a brick-and-mortar setting but complex when one therapist could theoretically have virtual clients all over the world. In the past, regulators have even suggested that patients who are traveling out of state might not be able to get treatment from their usual therapist. This has been changing for a while, thanks to rules like the Psychology Interjurisdictional Compact (known as  PSYPACT), a group set up by a number of psychology boards to create interstate systems for practicing teletherapy. COVID has also prompted states to  ease their restrictions temporarily. But change happens slowly.
  As those regulations change, one reason Borden said she's excited about teletherapy in general is that it can help patients find the right therapist, wherever they may be. "There's a shortage of behavioral health providers, particularly in rural areas," she said. Helping people find someone who understands them and their issues, she said, can be far more useful than just walking into the nearest door. (Talkspace has recently been making an effort to help Black patients find therapists who better understand their backgrounds.)
 Borden said the future isn't just virtual, but nor will the world go back to only face-to-face interactions when the pandemic ends. "My goal is to provide optionality so that people get care in the way they want to get care," she said. Whatever patients need, however they need it.
  The one thing all these modalities and options still have in common, though, is that they're a one-on-one experience — therapists often have many clients, but they're only working with one at a time. When the pandemic hit, Talkspace donated therapy hours to frontline workers, and Frank said he looked at ways to get more free services to people who needed it. But because every new client requires a therapist's time and attention, that approach just doesn't scale.
 So Talkspace came back to its basic goal: access. What better way to reach lots of people than on the most popular social platform on the planet? In March, Talkspace set up a number of Facebook groups. The most popular is " Free Public Support Group to Manage Coronavirus Anxiety," with nearly 3,000 members. They're frequently moderated by Talkspace therapists, who are paid for their time moderating the groups, but they're not therapy. The long disclaimer at the top of every group makes that very clear. Instead, they're a place for a lot more people to talk to each other and sometimes to a professional.
  The groups are filled with people looking for information or checking to make sure they're not alone in being totally unable to work or think or focus during these crazy times. The comments are, with rare exceptions, positive, helpful and kind. And while Talkspace therapists aren't paid to be there, they're often posting tips and asking questions.
 Talkspace's blog, and its Instagram and Twitter pages, have come to serve a similar purpose. Talkspace posts " Quick Ways to Reduce Stress" on Instagram, and people start chiming in with their own; it posts " Signs of PTSD" and people comment, "This is me." The company has hosted Q&As on Instagram, runs a series called "Talkspace Therapist Diaries," and has millions of blog readers every month.
 All that is well and good, but if Talkspace wanted to hit true scale, to give absolutely everyone access to virtual therapy, there's only one way forward: remove the human therapists from the equation entirely and replace them with AI chatbots. Therapy bots are very much  becoming a thing. Frank dismisses the whole idea out of hand. "People that talk about AI with regards to therapy or many other forms of health care," he said, "I think they're 50 years too early for that discussion — if at all."
  Talkspace has run some tests and tried some things around AI, Frank said, but the goal is to help the therapists, not replace them. Frank wants to use data to help train therapists on when to use video versus audio, or help psychaitrists figure out which medications to prescribe and when. Talkspace also has a risk algorithm it uses to flag particularly worrisome moments or messages so therapists can respond more quickly and correctly.
 AI can be a way to get more people more help, but Talkspace is mostly focused on how to make things easier on the therapists, many of whom are also still figuring out how to operate in this new multiplatform therapy space. "Our strategy and our belief is that the human is a far, far superior technology for the foreseeable future," Frank said. But humans and scale don't always mix. Talkspace recently relaxed its "guaranteed response time"  guidelines, which tell therapists how quickly they need to respond to a patient's messages, to give therapists more time without getting in trouble.
 Demand for Talkspace, and across the virtual therapy industry, continues to grow. Leibowitz said that one shortcut for tracking therapy needs is to track the economy — and as the U.S. heads into a recession, that need continues to go up. Within Talkspace, Frank said, the current system feels like it's working. Or, at least, it feels like the most Talkspace can do. "The network is stressed, our customer service is stressed," he said.
  Eventually, everyone seems to hope brick-and-mortar therapy comes back for those who need it. "My whole goal is to get everyone the kind of care they need, whatever that is," Cigna's Borden said. But with all those physical locations closed, the last few months have been a stress test for everything Talkspace, BetterHealth and the rest of the tech-forward therapy companies wanted to be — the kind of flexible, multiplatform therapy that felt like the future. "We've held up pretty well considering all the factors," Leibowitz said, "and I'm pretty proud we've been able to help people."
 Next in Protocol's Health Care Manual:    The future of staying healthy is sitting on your wrist
title: Talkspace和其他远程治疗公司正在努力扩大他们在大流行期间的业务，包括与保险公司和监管机构合作
title_en: Talkspace and other teletherapy companies are working to scale their businesses during the pandemic, including working with insurance companies and regulators

跳转到导航跳转搜索维纳斯花篮(曲霉菌)是海绵纲中的一种玻璃海绵。它是一种在太平洋深水中发现的海绵。与其他玻璃海绵一样，它们的骨架是由二氧化硅建造的，这在材料科学中引起了极大的兴趣，因为它们不需要加热就能形成它们的玻璃乳状物，这在某些方面使它们的性能优于人造光纤。[1][2]与其他海绵一样，它们以过滤海水捕获浮游生物为食。[3]。
这些海绵经常被发现是玻璃海绵虾的栖息地，通常是一对繁殖的海绵虾，由于它们的大小，它们通常无法离开海绵的格子。因此，它们生活在这些海绵内部和周围，在那里，虾与海绵之间表现出一种互惠互利的关系，直到它们死去。这可能影响了日本采用海绵作为永恒爱情的象征，在日本，海绵的骨骼被作为结婚礼物赠送。[4][5]。
在菲律宾群岛附近的一小片海域发现了维纳斯花篮。类似的物种出现在日本附近以及西太平洋和印度洋的其他地区。
胞体呈管状，弯曲，呈篮状，由三轴突针组成。身体上有许多孔，这些孔不是真正的Ostia，而是简单的顶缝。管系呈聚锥体型，Ostia与流入管沟通，后者通过管孔与放射管沟通，放射管通过管口向内通向海绵体，向外通向外口。
这些动物的身体结构是一个薄壁、圆柱形、花瓶状的管子，中间有一个很大的中庭。身体完全由6角硅质针状物形式的硅石组成，这就是为什么它们通常被称为玻璃海绵的原因。针状体由3条垂直的射线组成，形成6个点。针状体是海绵组织内的微小针状结构，为海绵提供结构支撑。它是海绵组织中针状体形式的组合，有助于识别该物种。在玻璃海绵的情况下，针状物编织在一起形成一个非常精细的网格，这给了海绵的身体一种在其他海绵物种中找不到的刚性，并允许玻璃海绵在水柱的很深的地方存活。
纤维光学研究人员对将海绵附着到海底的玻璃质纤维很感兴趣，这种纤维长5-20厘米(2-8英寸)，与人的头发一样细。[7][8]海绵从海水中提取硅酸并将其转化为二氧化硅，然后将其形成精细的玻璃纤维骨架。其他海绵，如橙色麻球海绵(Tethya Aurantium)也可以通过生物方式生产玻璃。目前的光纤制造工艺需要高温，并且会产生脆性光纤。在海绵的启发下，创造和排列这种纤维的低温过程可以提供对纤维光学特性的更多控制。这些纳米结构对于制造效率更高、成本更低的太阳能电池也有潜在的用处。[9]。
这些海绵骨架具有复杂的几何构型，这些几何构型因其刚度、屈服强度和最小裂纹扩展而被广泛研究。长度、有效厚度和半径相等但分布均匀的铝管(铝和玻璃弹性模量相似)的刚度是其刚度的1/100。[需要引用]

Jump to navigation  Jump to search  The  Venus' flower basket ( Euplectella aspergillum) is a  glass sponge in the  phylum  Porifera. It is a marine sponge found in the deep waters of the  Pacific ocean. As other glass sponges, they build their skeletons out of  silica, which is of great interest in  materials science as they do not require heat to form their glass latices, which in some ways makes their properties superior to manufactured  fiber optics.  [1]  [2] As other sponges, they feed by filtering sea water to capture  plankton.  [3]
 The sponges are often found to  house  glass sponge shrimp, usually a breeding pair, whom are typically unable to exit the sponge's lattice due to their size. Consequently, they live in and around these sponges, where the shrimp perform a  mutuallistic relationship with the sponge until they die. This may have influenced the adoption of the sponge as a symbol of undying love in  Japan, where the skeletons of these sponges are presented as  nuptial gifts.  [4]   [5]
   Venus' flower baskets are found in a small area of the sea nearby the Philippine Islands. Similar species occur near Japan and in other parts of the western Pacific Ocean and the Indian Ocean.
    The body is tubular, curved and basket-like and made up of triaxon spicules. The body is perforated by numerous apertures, which are not true ostia but simply parietal gaps. Syconoid type of canal system is present, where ostia communicate with incurrent canals, which communicates with radial canals through prosopyles which, in turn, open in to spongocoel and to outside through osculum.
 The body structure of these animals is a thin-walled, cylindrical, vase-shaped tube with a large central atrium. The body is composed entirely of silica in the form of 6-pointed siliceous spicules, which is why they are commonly known as glass sponges. The spicules are composed of 3 perpendicular rays giving them 6 points. Spicules are microscopic, pin-like structures within the sponge's tissues that provide structural support for the sponge. It is the combination of spicule forms within a sponge's tissues that helps identify the species. In the case of glass sponges the spicules "weave" together to form a very fine mesh which gives the sponge's body a rigidity not found in other sponge species and allows glass sponges to survive at great depths in the water column.
   The glassy fibers that attach the sponge to the ocean floor, 5–20 centimetres (2–8 in) long and thin as human hair, are of interest to  fiber optics researchers.  [7]  [8] The sponge extracts  silicic acid from seawater and converts it into  silica, then forms it into an elaborate skeleton of glass fibers. Other sponges such as the orange puffball sponge ( Tethya aurantium) can also produce glass biologically. The current manufacturing process for optical fibers requires high temperatures and produces a brittle fiber. A low-temperature process for creating and arranging such fibers, inspired by sponges, could offer more control over the optical properties of the fibers. These nano-structures are also potentially useful for the creation of more efficient, low-cost solar cells.  [9]
 These sponges skeletons have complex geometric configurations, which have been extensively studied for their stiffness, yield strength, and minimal crack propagation. An aluminum tube (aluminum and glass have similar elastic modulus) of equal length, effective thickness, and radius, but homogeneously distributed, has 1/100th the stiffness. [   citation needed]
title: 建造硅石骨架的海洋玻璃海绵
title_en: Marine glass sponge that builds silica skeletons

纸牌是几年前我在一台古老的Windows3.1笔记本电脑上玩的第一款电脑游戏。我从来没有真正实施过。当我说纸牌时，我实际上指的是Klondike的变体，我认为它是电脑玩家中最常见的。
这些天我喜欢C语言，因为它离机器很近，而且几乎所有人都可以随身携带，这让我有一种禅宗般的感觉。标准库不像Python、Java或JavaScript那样方便，但是我们不需要太多复杂的数据结构，所以我们可以使用自己的结构。
最初不会有图形版本，但基于终端字符的版本似乎是一个好主意，本着一次只做一件事的精神-先做一些游戏逻辑，然后再做一些用户界面，甚至可能是后来的GUI。
我不会为自己喜欢的项目做太多的前期计划，它们大多是在旅途中发展起来的。然而，在这个游戏中，计划是按照以下路线进行：
枚举{套装心脏，套装黑桃，套装俱乐部，套装钻石//♥♠♣♦}；枚举{RANK_A，RANK_2，RANK_3，RANK_4，RANK_5，RANK_6，RANK_7，RANK_8，RANK_9，RANK_10，RANK_J，RANK_Q，RANK_K}；类型定义结构卡{INT SUIT；INT RANK；}；
两张牌是交替颜色的吗？(因为列中的卡片颜色必须在红色和黑色之间交替)。
卡可以放在底座上吗？(A♥-&gt；2♥-&gt；3♥-&gt；.-&gt；Q♥-&gt；K♥)。
卡片可以放在底部吗？(交替且按顺序)(J♦-&gt；10♠-&gt；9♥-&gt；8♣-&gt；7♥)
int is_Black(卡片c){return C.Suit==Suit_Club||C.Suit==Suit_Spade；}int is_red(卡片c){Return C.Suit==Suit_Heart||C.Suit==Suit_Diamond；}int IS_Alternate_Color(卡片第一，卡片第二){Return IS_Black(第一)！=IS_Black(第二)；}int is_in_Sequence(卡片较低，卡片较高){返回较高。排名==较低。排名+1；}int Can_Be_Placed_Bottom(卡片父项，卡片子项){return is_Alternate_color(父项，子项)&amp；&amp；is_in_Sequence(子项，父项)；}int is_Same_Suit(卡片第一，卡片第二){返回第一。西装==第二件。Suit；}int Can_be_Placed_on_Foundation(卡片父项，卡片子项){return is_ame_Suit(父项，子项)&amp；&amp；is_in_Sequence(父项，子项)；}。
卡C5s=make_card(Suit_Spade，RANK_5)；卡C6H=MAKE_CARD(Suit_HARD，RANK_6)；printf(&#34；5s为黑色%d vs 1\n&#34；，is_Black(C5s))；printf(&#34；5s为红色%d vs 0\n&#34；，is_red(C5s))；printf(&#34；5s 6h为备用%d vs 0\n&#34；)；printf(&#34；5s 6h为备用%d vs 0\n&#34；，is_red(C5s))。
注意：如果您想要比在开发期间在main()函数中转储一些代码更正确地进行测试，那么在C中有很多单元测试框架。
让我们来定义我们的第一堆卡片--最初的52张卡片(股票)。
纸牌周围似乎还有几堆纸牌--例如，废纸牌(显露的卡片)、地基上的一堆纸牌和底部的几堆纸牌，我们称之为柱子。将桩的概念封装为具有相关功能的结构是有意义的。游戏板/游戏状态结构将包含所有这些堆。
#DEFINE CARD_COUNT 52 tyfinf struct card_node{card*value；struct card_node*next；}card_node；tyfinf struct pilt{card_node*head；int num_card；}pill；tyfinf struct Game_state{pil**pols；int pile_count；}Game_state；
堆**堆是指向堆的指针数组，也可以写为堆*堆[PILL_COUNT]。
我们可以用链表表示一堆牌的集合，或者只是假设不会有超过52张的牌堆，并使用一个数组作为后备存储和一个计数器。这样，以每堆更多的内存开销为代价。由于有一个已知的桩数：未翻转和已翻转的卡片组，4个基础，7根柱，总数为2+4+7=13个桩。在32位系统上，这最多是13*(卡的大小*)*card_count=13*4*52=2704字节开销，在PC上没有那么多，这可能是微控制器的一个因素。
另一方面，链表是一种传统的C结构，因此使用它们可能会更好。让我想想。
我们需要一套桩操作函数。Push/Pop处理卡片列表的末尾。对于处理列表开头的函数，我选择使用JavaScript命名法(Shift/UnShift)。
Java LinkedList使用addFirst、addLast、pollFirst、polllast、getFirst、getLast等名称，但它们更一致。
pileMake_pile()；void ush(堆*堆，card*card)；card*pop(堆*堆)；card*shift(堆*堆)；void unShift(堆*堆，card*card)；card*peek_card_at(堆*堆，int index)；card*peek(堆*堆)；card*peek_last(堆*堆)；void delete(堆*堆，card*card)；
这些函数的实现非常简单-我们必须链接或取消链接列表中的项目。我还选择使用非侵入式列表结构，节点类型为CARD_NODE，数据类型为CARD。
介入式容器和非介入式容器之间的区别在于，介入式容器中的值类型知道它们是某个集合的一部分，这意味着链接嵌入在结构中，例如：
在非侵入式列表中，值类型不嵌入链接，因此我们引入了具有指向card`值的指针的card_node节点类型。
这会影响列表操作函数的实现，性能侵入型列表在迭代时分配较少，取消引用也较少。使用非侵入式容器可以分离值和容器数据，我喜欢在软件中分离关注点(和职责)。
我也可能受到Java/C#等平台提供的数据结构是非侵入性的影响。
为了实例化结构，我使用了make_type的约定，它使用malloc()为它们分配内存。此内存必须稍后释放，这是销毁列表节点的函数的责任：POP/SHIFT/DELETE。由于纸牌中的所有52张牌碰巧都不会消失，我们不需要删除牌本身或游戏状态-当玩家退出游戏时，它们将被释放。
我一直试图一致地跟踪枚举中的项数。我喜欢将最后一个枚举项添加为ENUM_COUNT，由于枚举值在C中为零索引，因此它可以方便地解析为以前项的数量。
当然，您也可以#定义Suite_Count 5，但是您还应该记住在枚举增长或缩小时更改定义。
了解了基本函数后，让我们编写第一个填充初始纸牌的游戏手持设备函数。
void Fill_Deck(堆*堆){FOR(int rank=0；ank&lt；rank_count；rank++){for(int Suit=0；Suit&lt；Suit_count；Suit++){PUSH(PILL，MAKE_CARD_PTR(Suit，RANK)；}。
由于没有内置的列表混洗功能，我不得不使用自己的功能。它类似于Fisher-Yates Shuffle，但是我们在技术上不交换元素，而是将第一个元素插入到一个随机的位置。
void shashffle_堆(堆*堆){int shashffle_Times=堆-&gt；num_card*10；for(int i=0；i&lt；Shuffle_Times；i++){//取消卡片移位并插入到随机位置int idx=rand()%堆-&gt；num_card-1；card_ptrcard=Shift(堆)；INSERT(堆，卡，idx)；}}
一个流行的文本用户界面库被称为ncurses(新的curses)。我们可以通过使用它的Unicode版本ncursesw来获得对Unicode卡符号的支持，比如♥♠♣♦。这使我们能够将卡片表示为相当易读的10♠或J♥。
我们可以引入帮助器函数，例如RANK_to_charptr和Suit_to_charptr，我们将使用这些函数来打印卡片表示，并使用花色的Unicode字符代码：
const char*Suit_to_charptr(Int Suit){Switch(Suit){case Suit_Heart：return&#34；\u2665&#34；；case Suit_Spade：return&#34；\u2660&#34；；case Suit_Club：return&#34；\u2663&#34；；case Suit_Diamond：return&#34；\u2666&#34；.}}。
要在屏幕上的某个位置打印卡，我们使用MOVE(int row，int column)函数设置光标位置，然后使用printw ncurses函数进行打印，该函数的行为类似于printf。还有一些变体，比如mvprintw，它将移动光标和打印结合在一起。
现在我们需要在屏幕上布置堆积。对于纸牌玩家来说，它应该看起来很熟悉：
在这里，我还必须决定如何对成堆的卡片进行排序。我认为，第一张卡片主要是展示的那张-用于库存、废物和饲料堆放，这是有道理的。
我需要添加一个peek(堆*堆)函数来查看堆，因为这些堆只会显示最上面的卡片。
柱堆将按从上到下的顺序排列，所以最初只会显示最后一张牌，但我们可以从第一张牌画到最后一张牌。
呈现代码相当乏味，它使用字符串数组作为标题，选择任意大小(100)作为游戏终端宽度，然后遍历堆积如山并在屏幕上移动光标，直到全部布置完毕。
char*first_row_headers[]={&#34；Stock&#34；，&#34；waste&#34；，&#34；，&#34；Foundation 1&#34；，&#34；Foundation 2&#34；，&#34；Foundation 3&#34；，&#34；Foundation 4&#34；}；.//第一行标题int column_size=14；for(int i=0；i&lt；7；i++){Move(0，column_size*i)；printw(&#34；%s&#34；，first_row_headers[i])；}//第一行内容移动(1，0)；printw_card(peek(stock(State)；move(2，0)；printw_堆_size(stock(State))；.//Foundations for(int f=0；f&lt；Foundation_count；f++){int Foundation_。Move(1，(Foundation_1_Column+f)*Column_Size)；printw_card(peek(Foundation(state，f)；Move(2，(Foundation_1_Column+f)*Column_Size)；printw_堆_Size(Foundation(state，f))；}。
在开发这一点上，显示正面朝下的牌(未显示的牌，列中的大多数牌)的等级和花色是有意义的，表示为(Q♦)。我还将显露的旗帜(正面朝上)添加到卡片结构中，因为列中可能有多张正面朝上的卡片序列。
如果我们增加一点颜色，玩家将更容易区分4个♣和4个♥(四个黑桃和四个红心)。幸运的是，Ncurses支持终端中的颜色。我用了吉姆·霍尔的教程来快速掌握基础知识。
游戏机只支持八种基本颜色-黑、红、绿、黄、蓝、品红、青、白。然后，我们必须使用init_air(索引、前景、背景)定义颜色对。您还可以将-1作为颜色传递给此函数，以使用默认值。
要在打印期间实际使用该颜色对，请使用ATTRON(COLOR_Pair(Int Pair))打开颜色属性。稍后应该通过相应的attroff()调用将其关闭。
我们可以选择使用基于文本的控件，它(在移动卡片的情况下)具有源目标的形式，因此，例如，c3c4表示从第3列中取出1张卡片，并将其放在第4列。我们还需要从库存中抽出一张卡片，因此这可能是命令s。要移动抽出的卡片(从废品中)，我们将使用w。因为有时需要移动多张卡片，所以可以使用类似3c1c5-从第1列中取出3张卡片并将其放入第4列的命令来解决。由于有时需要移动多张卡片，因此可以使用类似3c1c5-从第1列和第4列中取出三张卡片的命令来解决。
有些事情，应该是不可能的。将多张牌从一列移到一个基础是没有意义的，因为它们不会按照同一花色的升序排列。将多张卡片从废品移入一列也是不允许的。从库存中抽出多张牌可能是可能的，但让我们忽略它，稍后再添加它。让我们也禁止将卡从基金会转移到其他地方。
关于如何用C语言解析用户输入，我们有多种选择。我最初想手动解析它，逐个字符并跟踪状态，或者将scanf函数与多个输入模板一起使用，例如%c%d%c%d，用于c3f1之类的东西。scanf系列函数返回特定转换的数量，因此我们可以检查返回值是否成功并级联检查。
使用scanf选项时，应该按照从最具体到最不具体的顺序准备模板，我们最终得到四种特定模式：
%dC%d c%d-&gt；3c4 c5%dC%c%d-&gt；c3 F1/c7 c2w%c%d-&gt；w c4/w F3s-&gt；s。
由于解析函数parsed_input parse_input(char*command)应该返回多个值，让我们将其封装在一个结构中：
tyfinf struct parsed_input{char source；char destination；int source_index；int target_index；int source_mount；int SUCCESS；}parsed_input；
解析函数逐个尝试模式，同时填充源/目的地，因为某些模式暗示了它们-例如，Pattern_STOCK表示源是库存堆，而目的地是未定义的。
